content: One possible condition for the three points (a, b), (b, a) and (a2, $$-$$ b2) to be collinear is
explanation:
Let A(a, b), B(b, a), C(a2, $$-$$ b2) be three points which are collinear.

$$\therefore$$ $$\left| {\matrix{ a & b & 1 \cr b & a & 1 \cr {{a^2}} & { - {b^2}} & 1 \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1( - {b^3} - {a^3}) - 1( - a{b^2} - {a^2}b) + 1({a^2} - {b^2}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow - ({a^3} + {b^3}) + ab(a + b) + (a + b)(a - b) = 0$$

$$ = - (a + b)({a^2} + {b^2} - ab) + ab(a + b) + (a + b)(a - b) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a + b)( - {a^2} - {b^2} + ab + ab + a - b) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a + b)( - {a^2} - {b^2} + 2ab + a - b) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a + b)( - ({a^2} + {b^2} - 2ab) + (a - b)) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a + b)( - {(a - b)^2} + (a - b)) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a + b)(a - b)( - (a - b) + 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow ({a^2} - {b^2})(1 - a + b) = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ either $${a^2} - {b^2} = 0$$ or $$1 - a + b = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1 + b$$.

content: The distance between the lines $$5x - 12y + 65 = 0$$ and $$5x - 12y - 39 = 0$$ is
explanation:
Given equations of lines are $$5x - 12y + 65 = 0$$ and $$5x - 12y - 39 = 0$$

Since given lines are parallel

$$\therefore$$ Perpendicular distance $$ = {{|65 + 39|} \over {\sqrt {{{(5)}^2} + {{(12)}^2}} }}$$

$$ = {{104} \over {\sqrt {25 + 144} }} = {{104} \over {13}} = 8$$.

content: The co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular from (a, 0) on the line $$y = mx + {a \over m}$$ are
explanation:
Slope of given line = m

Equation of line is $$y = mx + {a \over m}$$

Slope of perpendicular line $$PQ = {{ - 1} \over m}$$

Equation of perpendicular line is

$$y - 0 = {{ - 1} \over m}(x - a)$$ or $$my = - x + a$$

$$y = - {1 \over m}x + {a \over m}$$ ..... (i)

$$y = mx + {a \over m}$$ ..... (ii)

Subtracting (i) from (ii)

$$\left( {m + {1 \over m}} \right)x = 0$$

Putting x = 0 in (i), $$y = {a \over m}$$

So point $$Q\left( {0,{a \over m}} \right)$$.

content: If C is a point on the line segment joining A($$-$$3, 4) and B(2, 1) such that AC = 2BC, then the coordinate of C is
explanation:
A($$-$$3, 4) and B(2, 1)

AC = 2BC

or, $${{AC} \over {BC}} = 2:1$$

Using section formula,

$$h = {{2\,.\,2 + 1\,.\,( - 3)} \over {2 + 1}} = {1 \over 3}$$

$$k = {{2\,.\,1 + 1\,.\,4} \over {2 + 1}} = 2$$

$$\therefore$$ Coordinates of C are $$\left( {{1 \over 3},2} \right)$$

content: The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular from (0, 0) upon the line x + y = 2 are
explanation:
Let coordinates of foot P are (h, k)

$$\therefore$$ Slope of OP = k/h

Slope of given line = $$-$$1

$$\because$$ LM $$\bot$$ OP

$$\therefore$$ $${k \over h} \times - 1 = - 1$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ k = h ..... (i)

Also point P lies on the given line

$$\therefore$$ k + h = 2 ..... (ii)

Solving (i) and (ii), we get h = 1, k = 1

So coordinates of foot are (1, 1).

content: A line through the point A(2, 0) which makes an angle of 30$$^\circ$$ with the positive direction of x-axis is rotated about A in clockwise direction through an angle 15$$^\circ$$. Then the equation of the straight line in the new position is
explanation:
Equation of line which is passing through (x1, y1) and angle of inclination $$\theta$$ with +ve x axis in anti-clockwise is

$$y - {y_1} = \tan \theta (x - {x_1})$$

$$\therefore$$ $$(y - 0) = \tan 15^\circ (x - 2)$$

$$[\tan 15^\circ = \tan (45^\circ - 30^\circ ) = {{\tan 45^\circ - \tan 30^\circ } \over {1 + \tan 45^\circ \tan 30^\circ }} = {{\sqrt 3 - 1} \over {\sqrt 3 + 1}} = 2 - \sqrt 3 ]$$

$$ \Rightarrow (2 - \sqrt 3 )x - y - 4 + 2\sqrt 3 = 0$$

content: If C is the reflection of A(2, 4) in x-axis and B is the reflection of C in y-axis, then $$|AB|$$ is
explanation:
Reflection of point (h, k) about x-axis is (h, $$-$$k)

Reflection of point (h, k) about y-axis is ($$-$$h, k)

$$\therefore$$ Reflection point C of A(2, 4) in x-axis is (2, $$-$$4) and reflection point B of C(2, $$-$$4) in y-axis is ($$-$$2, $$-$$4)

$$\therefore$$ $$|AB| = \sqrt {{{(2 + 2)}^2} + {{(4 + 4)}^2}} = 4\sqrt 5 $$

content: The number of points on the line x + y = 4 which are unit distance apart from the line 2x + 2y = 5 is
explanation:
Since x + y = 4 and 2x + 2y = 5 are parallel.

Take (4, 0) on the line x + y = 4

Distance of (4, 0) from the line 2x + 2y $$-$$ 5 = 0 is

$${{|2\,.\,4 + 2\,.\,0 - 5|} \over {\sqrt {{2^2} + {2^2}} }} = {3 \over {2\sqrt 2 }} = {{3\sqrt 2 } \over 4} > 1$$ (unit distance)

$$\because$$ Both lines are parallel and at a distance greater than unity

$$\therefore$$ There is no point on the line x + y = 4.

content: The point ($$-$$4, 5) is the vertex of a square and one of its diagonals is 7x $$-$$ y + 8 = 0. The equation of the other diagonal is
explanation:
Point ($$-$$4, 5) does not lie on the diagonal 7x $$-$$ y + 8 = 0, so point will lie on the other diagonal, also diagonals are perpendicular

$$\therefore$$ Slope of other diagonal = $$-$$1/7

$$\therefore$$ Equation of other diagonal is

$$y - 5 = - {1 \over 7}(x + 4) \Rightarrow 7y + x = 31$$

content: The straight line 3x + y = 9 divides the line segment joining the points (1, 3) and (2, 7) in the ratio
explanation:
Let 3x + y = 9 ..... (1)

divides the join of (1, 3) & (2, 7) in the ratio m : n (interlally) at P(x', y')

$$\therefore$$ $$x' = {{m\,.\,2 + n\,.\,1} \over {m + n}}$$, $$y' = {{m\,.\,7 + n\,.\,3} \over {m + n}}$$

$$\because$$ P(x', y') lies on (1) $$\therefore$$ 3x' + y' = 9

$$ \Rightarrow 3\left( {{{2m + n} \over {m + n}}} \right) + \left( {{{7m + 3n} \over {m + n}}} \right) = 9$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ 4m = 3n $$\therefore$$ m : n = 3 : 4

Reqd. ratio = 3 : 4 (internally)

Reqd. ratio = $$ - {{3(1) + (3) - 9} \over {3(2) + (7) - 9}} = 3:4$$

content: If the three points (3q, 0) (0, 3p) and (1, 1) are collinear then which one is true?
explanation:
(3q, 0) (0, 3p), (1, 1) collinear

$$\Rightarrow$$ Slope of line joining (3q, 0) & (1, 1) = Slope of line joining (0, 3p) & (1, 1)

$$\Rightarrow$$ $${{1 - 0} \over {1 - 3q}} = {{1 - 3p} \over {1 - 0}} \Rightarrow 1 - 3p - 3q + 9pq = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow p + q = 3pq$$

$$\therefore$$ $${1 \over p} + {1 \over q} = 3$$

content: The equations $$y = \pm \sqrt {3x} $$, y = 1 are the sides of

content: The equations of the lines through (1, 1) and making angles of 45$$^\circ$$ with the line x + y = 0 are
explanation:
Any line through (1, 1) is

content: The equation of the locus of the point of intersection of the straight lines $$x\sin \theta + (1 - \cos \theta )y = a\sin \theta $$ and $$x\sin \theta - (1 + \cos \theta )y + a\sin \theta = 0$$ is
explanation:
Equations can be written as

$$x + (\cos ec\theta - \cot \theta )y = a$$ & $$x - (\cos ec\theta + \cot \theta )y + a = 0$$

i.e., $$(\cos ec\theta - \cot \theta )y = a - x$$ & $$(\cos ec\theta + \cot \theta )y = x + a$$

On multiplying, $${y^2} = {a^2} - {x^2} \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}$$

content: Consider three points $P(\cos \alpha, \sin \beta), Q(\sin \alpha, \cos \beta)$ and $R(0,0)$, where $0<\alpha, \beta<\frac{\pi}{4}$. Then

options.content: $P$ lies on the line segment $R Q$.
$Q$ lies on the line segment $P R$.
$R$ lies on the line segment $P Q$.

explanation:
We check collinearity by comparing the slopes of the three pairs:

Slope of PR

$$m_{PR}=\frac{y_P-y_R}{x_P-x_R}=\frac{\sin\beta-0}{\cos\alpha-0} =\frac{\sin\beta}{\cos\alpha}>0\quad(\text{since }0<\beta<\tfrac\pi4).$$

Slope of RQ

$$m_{RQ}=\frac{y_Q-y_R}{x_Q-x_R}=\frac{\cos\beta-0}{\sin\alpha-0} =\frac{\cos\beta}{\sin\alpha}>0\quad(\text{since }0<\alpha<\tfrac\pi4).$$

Slope of PQ

$$m_{PQ}=\frac{\cos\beta-\sin\beta}{\sin\alpha-\cos\alpha} =\frac{\,\cos\beta-\sin\beta\,}{-(\cos\alpha-\sin\alpha)} =-\frac{\cos\beta-\sin\beta}{\cos\alpha-\sin\alpha}<0$$

because in $(0,\tfrac\pi4)$ we have $\cos t>\sin t$, so both $\cos\beta-\sin\beta$ and $\cos\alpha-\sin\alpha$ are positive.

Since two of the slopes are positive and one is negative, no two of the three points lie on the same straight line. Hence

Option D: $P,\;Q,\;R$ are non-collinear.

content: The line parallel to the $x$-axis passing through the intersection of the lines $a x+2 b y+3 b=0$ and $b x-2 a y-3 a=0$ where $(a, b) \neq(0,0)$ is

options.content: above $x$-axis at a distance $\frac{3}{2}$ from it.
above $x$-axis at a distance $\frac{2}{3}$ from it.
below $x$-axis at a distance $\frac{3}{2}$ from it.
below $x$-axis at a distance $\frac{2}{3}$ from it.

explanation:
Given the two lines

$$ \begin{aligned} & a x+2 b y+3 b=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & b x-2 a y-3 a=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

To find the point of intersection, we can solve these simultaneous equations. Multiply Eq. (i) by $a$ and Eq. (ii) by $b$

$$ \begin{aligned} & & a(a x+2 b y+3 b) & =0 \\ & \Rightarrow & a^2 x+2 a b y+3 a b & =0 \\ & & b(b x-2 a y-3 a) & =0 \\\Rightarrow & & b^2 x-2 a b y-3 a b & =0 \end{aligned} $$

Adding these two equations.

$$ \begin{array}{r} \left(a^2 x+2 a b y+3 a b\right)+\left(b^2 x-2 a b y-3 a b\right)=0 \\ \left(a^2+b^2\right) x=0 \end{array} $$

Since, $(a, b) \neq(0,0)$, it implies $a^2+b^2 \neq 0$. Therefore, $x=0$.

Subsitute $x=0$ into the first equation.

$$ \begin{aligned} & & a(0)+2 b y+3 b & =0 \\ & & 2 b y+3 b & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & b(2 y+3) & =0 \end{aligned} $$

Since, $(a, b) \neq(0,0)$ there are two possibilities If $b \neq 0$, then $2 y+3=0 \Rightarrow y=-\frac{3}{2}$.

If $b=0$, then from the original equations, since $(a, b) \neq(0,0)$, we must have $a \neq 0$.

The first equation becomes $a x=0$, which gives $x=0$.

The second equation becomes $-2 a y-3 a=0$, which, since $a \neq 0$, simplifies to $-2 y-3=0$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad y=-\frac{3}{2} $$

$\Rightarrow$ This line is located below the $X$-axis at a distance of $\frac{3}{2}$ from it.

content: If $$(1,5)$$ be the midpoint of the segment of a line between the line $$5 x-y-4=0$$ and $$3 x+4 y-4=0$$, then the equation of the line will be

In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$, co-ordinates of $$\mathrm{A}$$ are $$(1,2)$$ and the equation of the medians through $$\mathrm{B}$$ and C are $$x+\mathrm{y}=5$$ and $$x=4$$ respectively. Then the midpoint of $$\mathrm{BC}$$ is
A, B are fixed points with coordinates (0, a) and (0, b) (a > 0, b > 0). P is variable point (x, 0) referred to rectangular axis. If the angle $$\angle$$APB is maximum, then
explanation:
$$\angle A P B=\alpha=\angle A P O-\angle B P O$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a}{x}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{b}{x}\right) $$

For maximum value of $$\alpha$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d \alpha}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+\frac{a^2}{x^2}} \times \frac{-a}{x^2}-\frac{1}{1+\frac{b^2}{x^2}} \times \frac{-b}{x^2}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \frac{-a}{x^2+a^2}+\frac{b}{x^2+b^2}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad b\left(x^2+a^2\right)=a\left(x^2+b^2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \quad x^2=a b \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation $${r^2}{\cos ^2}\left( {\theta - {\pi \over 3}} \right) = 2$$ represents
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } r^2 \cos ^2\left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2 . \\ & \Rightarrow \quad r^2\left(\cos \left(\theta-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right)^2=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad r^2\left(\cos \theta \cos \frac{\pi}{3}+\sin \theta \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right)^2=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \quad r^2\left(\frac{\cos \theta}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin \theta\right)^2=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{4}(r \cos \theta+\sqrt{3} r \sin \theta)^2=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{lrl} \Rightarrow & (x+\sqrt{3} y)^2=8 \\ \Rightarrow & (x+\sqrt{3} y)^2-(2 \sqrt{2})^2 & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & (x+\sqrt{3} y+2 \sqrt{2})(x+\sqrt{3} y-2 \sqrt{2}) & =0 \end{array}$$

Which represents a pair of lines.

content: If $$4{a^2} + 9{b^2} - {c^2} + 12ab = 0$$, then the family of straight lines $$ax + by + c = 0$$ is concurrent at
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{lr} \text { } & 4 a^2+9 b^2-c^2+12 a b=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (2 a)^2+(3 b)^2+2(2 a)(3 b)-c^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad(2 a+3 b)^2-c^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (2 a+3 b-c)(2 a+3 b+c)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (-2 a-3 b+c)(2 a+3 b+c)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -2 a-3 b+c=0 \text { or } 2 a+3 b+c=0 \end{array}$$

Comparing with $$a x+b y+c=0$$

$$(x, y)=(-2,-3) \text { or }(2,3)$$

So, given family of lines are concurrent at $$(2,3)$$ or $$(-2,-3)$$.

content: The straight lines $$x + 2y - 9 = 0,3x + 5y - 5 = 0$$ and $$ax + by - 1 = 0$$ are concurrent if the straight line $$35x - 22y + 1 = 0$$ passes through the point
explanation:
If $$x+2 y-9=0,3 x+5 y-5=0, a x+b y-1=0$$ are concurrent, then

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -9 \\ 3 & 5 & -5 \\ a & b & -1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(-5+5 b)-2(-3+5 a)-9(3 b-5 a)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad-5+5 b+6-10 a-27 b+45 a=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 35 a-22 b+1=0 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with $$35 x-22 y+1=0$$

$$(x, y)=(a, b)$$

$$\therefore \quad 35 x-22 y+1$$ passes through $$(a, b)$$.

content: The locus of points (x, y) in the plane satisfying $${\sin ^2}x + {\sin ^2}y = 1$$ consists of

options.content: infinitely many circles that are all centered at the origin

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } \sin ^2 x+\sin ^2 y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin ^2 y=1-\sin ^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin ^2 y=\cos ^2 x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin y= \pm \cos x \\ & \text { If } \sin y=\cos x=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=n \pi+(-1)^n\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right), n \in Z \end{aligned}$$

Which represents equation of line with slope $$\pm 1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{and if} \quad & \sin y=-\cos x=-\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) \\ & =\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=n \pi+(-1)^n\left(x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right), n \in Z \\ \end{aligned}$$

which represents equation of line with slope $$\pm 1$$

So, there are infinitely many lines with slope $$\pm 1$$.

content: If the algebraic sum of the distances from the points (2, 0), (0, 2) and (1, 1) to a variable straight line be zero, then the line passes through the fixed point
explanation:
Let the equation of the line be $$ax + by + c = 0$$

According to the given condition, we have

$$\left| {{{2a + c} \over {\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}} \right| + \left| {{{2b + c} \over {\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}} \right| + \left| {{{a + b + c} \over {\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}} \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \, \pm {{2a + c} \over {\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}\, \pm \,\left( {{{2b + c} \over {\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}} \right)\, \pm \,\left( {{{a + b + c} \over {\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}} \right) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2a + c + 2b + c + a + b + c = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3a + 3b + 3c = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow a + b + c = 0$$

Comparing the line $$ax + by + c = 0$$ and $$a + b + c = 0$$ and get the fixed point.

$$\therefore$$ The required fixed point is (1, 1).

content: If a > 0, b > 0 then the maximum area of the parallelogram whose three vertices are O(0, 0), A(a cos$$\theta$$, b sin$$\theta$$) and B(a cos$$\theta$$, $$-$$ b sin$$\theta$$) is
explanation:
We have vertices of parallelogram
O(0, 0), A(a cos$$\theta$$, b sin$$\theta$$) and B(a cos$$\theta$$, $$-$$ b sin$$\theta$$)


Here, OA = OB

$$\therefore$$ Area of parallelogram = 2 $$\times$$ area of $$\Delta$$OAB

$$ = 2 \times \left( {{1 \over 2} \times OP \times AB} \right)$$

$$ = 2 \times {1 \over 2} \times a\cos \theta \times 2b\sin \theta $$

$$ = 2 \times ab(\sin \theta \cos \theta )$$

$$ = ab\sin 2\theta $$

$$\therefore$$ Area will be maximum when $$\theta = {\pi \over 4}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Maximum Area = ab when $$\theta = {\pi \over 4}$$

content: Let A be the fixed point (0, 4) and B be a moving point on X-axis. Let M be the midpoint of AB and let the perpendicular bisector of AB meets the Y-axis at R. The locus of the midpoint P of MR is
explanation:
Let point B = (2p, 0)


$$\therefore$$ Coordinates of M are (p, 2)

Now, Slope of AB = $${{4 - 0} \over {0 - 2p}} = - {2 \over p}$$

$$\therefore$$ Slope of MR = $${p \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Equations of MR = $$y - 2 = {p \over 2}(x - p)$$

On putting x = 0, we get

$$y - 2 = - {{{p^2}} \over 2} \Rightarrow y = 2 - {{{p^2}} \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Coordinates of R is $$\left( {0,2 - {{{p^2}} \over 2}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ Coordinates of P is $$\left( {{p \over 2},{{2 - {{{p^2}} \over 2} + 2} \over 2}} \right)$$

i.e. $$\left( {{p \over 2},2 - {{{p^2}} \over 4}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ $$y = 2 - {x^2} \Rightarrow y + {x^2} = 2$$

content: A moving line intersects the lines x + y = 0 and x $$-$$ y = 0 at the points A, B respectively such that the area of the triangle with vertices (0, 0), A and B has a constant area C. The locus of the mid-point AB is given by the equation
explanation:
Let coordinates of A are ($$\alpha$$, $$-$$ $$\alpha$$) and coordinates of B are ($$\beta$$, $$\beta$$)

$$\beta$$ + $$\alpha$$ = 2h and $$\beta$$ $$-$$ $$\alpha$$ = 2k

Now, Area of $$\Delta AOB = {1 \over 2} \times OA \times OB$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\sqrt {2{\alpha ^2}} \sqrt {2{\beta ^2}} = \left| {\alpha \beta } \right| = c$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2}{\beta ^2} = {c^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{\alpha ^2}{\beta ^2} = 16{c^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\{ {(\beta + \alpha )^2} - {(\beta - \alpha )^2}\} ^2} = 16{c^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(4{h^2} - 4{k^2})^2} = 16{c^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {h^2} - {k^2} = {c^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Locus of the mid point P of MR is $${({x^2} - {y^2})^2} = {c^2}$$

y = x satisfies both lines.

content: A straight line through the point (3, $$-$$2) is inclined at an angle 60$$^\circ$$ to the line $$\sqrt 3 x + y = 1$$. If it intersects the X-axis, then its equation will be

$$\eqalign{

& \Rightarrow \sqrt 3 (1 - \sqrt 3 {m_1}) = {m_1} + \sqrt 3 \cr
& \Rightarrow \sqrt 3 - 3{m_1} = {m_1} + \sqrt 3 \cr

& \Rightarrow {m_1} = 0 \cr} $$		 $$\eqalign{

& \Rightarrow 3{m_1} - {m_1} = 2\sqrt 3 \cr

& \Rightarrow {m_1} = \sqrt 3 \cr} $$

content: If the point of intersection of the lines 2ax + 4ay + c = 0 and 7bx + 3by $$-$$ d = 0 lies in the 4th quadrant and is equidistant from the two axes, where a, b, c and d are non-zero numbers, then ad : bc equals to

explanation:

which is a circle.

content: Transforming to parallel axes through a point (p, q), the equation $$2{x^2} + 3xy + 4{y^2} + x + 18y + 25 = 0$$ becomes $$2{x^2} + 3xy + 4{y^2} = 1$$. Then,
explanation:

$$ \Rightarrow {d_1} - {d_2}$$

$$ = {{13(3h + 4k - 5) - 5(12h + 5k - 7)} \over {65}}$$

$$ = {{39h + 52k - 65 - 60h - 25k + 35} \over {65}}$$

$$ = {{ - 21h + 27k - 30} \over {65}}$$

$$ = {{ - 21\left( {{{9k - 10} \over 7}} \right) + 27k - 30} \over {65}}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ = {{ - 27k + 30 + 27k - 30} \over {65}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {d_1} - {d_2} = 0 \Rightarrow {d_1} = {d_2}$$

content: Let $$\Gamma$$ be the curve $$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{be}^{-x / a}$$ & $$\mathrm{L}$$ be the straight line $$\frac{x}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{b}}=1$$ where $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbb{R}$$. Then

options.content: $$\mathrm{L}$$ touches the curve $$\Gamma$$ at the point where the curve crosses the axis of $$y$$.
$$\mathrm{L}$$ does not touch the curve at the point where the curve crosses the axis of $$\mathbf{y}$$.
$$\Gamma$$ touches the axis of $$x$$ at a point.

explanation:
Let's analyze the given curve and line to determine the correct statements.

The curve is given by:

$$\Gamma : y = be^{-x/a}$$

The straight line is given by:

$$L : \frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1$$

First, let's find where the curve $$\Gamma$$ crosses the y-axis. This occurs when $$x=0$$:

$$y = be^{0} = b$$

So, the curve crosses the y-axis at the point $$(0, b)$$.

Next, let’s check whether the line $$L$$ touches the curve $$\Gamma$$ at this point. Substituting $$x = 0$$ and $$y = b$$ into the equation of the line $$L$$:

$$\frac{0}{a} + \frac{b}{b} = 1$$

This is simplified to:

$$0 + 1 = 1$$

Thus, the point $$(0, b)$$ lies on the line $$L$$.

To determine if the line is tangent to the curve at this point, we need to check if they have the same slope at that point. First, find the derivative of the curve $$\Gamma$$ with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}(be^{-x/a}) = b \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{a}e^{-x/a}\right) = -\frac{b}{a}e^{-x/a}$$

At the point $$(0, b)$$, the derivative (slope of the curve) is:

$$\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -\frac{b}{a}e^{0} = -\frac{b}{a}$$

Now, find the slope of the line $$L$$. The line equation $$\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1$$ can be rearranged to the slope-intercept form:

$$\frac{y}{b} = -\frac{x}{a} + 1$$

or:

$$y = -\frac{b}{a}x + b$$

The slope of the line $$L$$ is:

$$-\frac{b}{a}$$

Since both the curve $$\Gamma$$ and the line $$L$$ have the same slope $$-\frac{b}{a}$$ at the point $$(0, b)$$, the line is tangent to the curve at that point.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A: $$\mathrm{L}$$ touches the curve $$\Gamma$$ at the point where the curve crosses the axis of $$y$$.

Finally, for completeness, let’s evaluate the other options:

Option B: This is incorrect because $$\mathrm{L}$$ does indeed touch the curve at the point where the curve crosses the y-axis.

Option C: The curve $$\Gamma$$ never touches the x-axis because as $$x \to \infty$$, $$y \to 0$$, but it never equals zero.

Option D: This is correct because as analyzed, the curve $$\Gamma$$ asymptotically approaches the x-axis but does not touch it.

content: A square with each side equal to '$$a$$' above the $$x$$-axis and has one vertex at the origin. One of the sides passing through the origin makes an angle $$\alpha$$ $$\left(0<\alpha< \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$ with the positive direction of the axis. Equation of the diagonals of the square
If $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ is an isosceles triangle and the coordinates of the base points are $$B(1,3)$$ and $$C(-2,7)$$. The coordinates of $$A$$ can be
explanation:
To determine the possible coordinates of the vertex $$A$$ in an isosceles triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ with base endpoints $$B(1,3)$$ and $$C(-2,7)$$, we need to find the coordinates that satisfy the property that $$\mathrm{AB} = \mathrm{AC}$$.

Let's calculate the distance between $$B(1,3)$$ and potential coordinates of $$A$$, as well as between $$C(-2,7)$$ and potential coordinates of $$A$$.

1. Option A: $$A(1,6)$$

$$ \mathrm{AB} = \sqrt{(1 - 1)^2 + (6 - 3)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 9} = \sqrt{9} = 3 $$

$$ \mathrm{AC} = \sqrt{(1 - (-2))^2 + (6 - 7)^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1} = \sqrt{10} $$

Since $$\mathrm{AB} \neq \mathrm{AC}$$, Option A is incorrect.

2. Option B: $$A\left(-\frac{1}{8}, 5\right)$$

$$ \mathrm{AB} = \sqrt{\left(1 - \left(-\frac{1}{8}\right)\right)^2 + (5 - 3)^2} = \sqrt{\left(1 + \frac{1}{8}\right)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{8}{8} + \frac{1}{8}\right)^2 + 4} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{9}{8}\right)^2 + 4} = \sqrt{\frac{81}{64} + \frac{256}{64}} = \sqrt{\frac{337}{64}} = \sqrt{5.27} $$

$$ \mathrm{AC} = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{1}{8} - (-2)\right)^2 + (5 - 7)^2} = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{1}{8} + 2\right)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{15}{8}\right)^2 + 4} = \sqrt{\frac{225}{64} + \frac{256}{64}} = \sqrt{\frac{481}{64}} = \sqrt{7.52} $$

Since $$\mathrm{AB} \neq \mathrm{AC}$$, Option B is incorrect.

3. Option C: $$A\left(\frac{5}{6}, 6\right)$$

$$ \mathrm{AB} = \sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{5}{6}\right)^2 + (6 - 3)^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{6}{6} - \frac{5}{6}\right)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^2 + 9} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{36} + \frac{324}{36}} = \sqrt{\frac{325}{36}} = \sqrt{9.03} $$

$$ \mathrm{AC} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{5}{6} - (-2)\right)^2 + (6 - 7)^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{5}{6} + \frac{12}{6}\right)^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^2 + 1} = \sqrt{\frac{289}{36} + \frac{36}{36}} = \sqrt{\frac{325}{36}} = \sqrt{9.03} $$

Since $$\mathrm{AB} = \mathrm{AC}$$, Option C is correct.

4. Option D: $$A\left(-7, \frac{1}{8}\right)$$

$$ \mathrm{AB} = \sqrt{(1 - (-7))^2 + \left(3 - \frac{1}{8}\right)^2} = \sqrt{8^2 + \left(\frac{24}{8} - \frac{1}{8}\right)^2} = \sqrt{64 + \left(\frac{23}{8}\right)^2} = \sqrt{64 + \frac{529}{64}} = \sqrt{\frac{4096 + 529}{64}} = \sqrt{\frac{4625}{64}} = \sqrt{72.27} $$

$$ \mathrm{AC} = \sqrt{\left(-7 - (-2)\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{8} - 7\right)^2} = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{8} - \frac{56}{8}\right)^2} = \sqrt{25 + \left(-\frac{55}{8}\right)^2} = \sqrt{25 + \frac{3025}{64}} = \sqrt{\frac{1600 + 3025}{64}} = \sqrt{\frac{4625}{64}} = \sqrt{72.27} $$

Since $$\mathrm{AB} = \mathrm{AC}$$, Option D is also correct.

Therefore, the correct coordinates of $$A$$ can be Option C: $$A\left(\frac{5}{6}, 6\right)$$ or Option D: $$A\left(-7, \frac{1}{8}\right)$$.

content: A rectangle ABCD has its side parallel to the line y = 2x and vertices A, B, D are on lines y = 1, x = 1 and x = $$-$$1 respectively. The coordinate of C can be
explanation:
Since, side of rectangle $$A B C D$$ is parallel to line $$y=2 x$$

$$\therefore \text { Let equation of } A B \text { is } y=2 x+c$$ ..... (i)

Since, A is on line $$y=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad 1=2 x+c \\ \Rightarrow & \quad x=\frac{1-c}{2} \\ \therefore & \quad A \equiv\left(\frac{1-c}{2}, 1\right) \end{aligned}$$

Since, $$B$$ is on line $$x=1$$

$$\begin{array}{lc} \therefore & y=2+c \\ \therefore & B=(1,2+c) \end{array}$$

Now, equation of $$D A$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & y-1=\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(x-\frac{1-c}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 2 y-2=-x+\frac{1-c}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & x+2 y=2+\frac{1-c}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Since, $$D$$ is on $$x=-1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore & -1+2 y & =2+\frac{1-c}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & & 2 y & =3+\frac{1-c}{2}=\frac{7-c}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & & y & =\frac{7-c}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad D \equiv\left(-1, \frac{7-c}{4}\right)$$

Let co-ordinates of $$c$$ are $$(\alpha, \beta)$$

Here, mid-point of $$A C=$$ mid-point of $$B D$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\alpha+\frac{1-c}{2}}{2}, \frac{\beta+1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{1-1}{2}, \frac{2+c+\frac{7-c}{4}}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{2 \alpha+1-c}{4}, \frac{\beta+1}{2}\right)=\left(0, \frac{3 c+15}{8}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{cc} \Rightarrow & \frac{2 \alpha+1-c}{4}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \alpha+1-c=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \alpha+1=c \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

and $$\quad\frac{\beta+1}{2}=\frac{3 c+15}{8}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & 4 \beta+4=3 c+15 \\ \Rightarrow & 4 \beta-11=3 c \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{4 \beta-11}{3}=c \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{array}$$

From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

$$2 \alpha+1=\frac{4 \beta-11}{3}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & 6 \alpha+3=4 \beta-11 \\ \Rightarrow & 6 \alpha-4 \beta=-14 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 \alpha-2 \beta=-7 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore$$ Locus of $$c$$ is $$3 x-2 y=-7$$ ..... (iv)

From given options (a) and (c), $$(3,8)$$ and $$(-3,-1)$$ satisfied (iv).

explanation:
Equation of straight line having intercepts on the coordinate axes are a and b, are

Given, $$a + b = - 1 \Rightarrow b = - (a + 1)$$

Line (i) passing through the point (4, 3).

$$ \Rightarrow {a^2} + a - a = 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow a = \pm 2$$

When $$a = 2$$, then $$b = - 3$$

and when $$a = - 2$$, then $$b = 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ Equation of straight line are $${x \over 2} - {y \over 3} = 1,{x \over { - 2}} + {y \over 1} = 1$$

explanation:

So, h + k + 1 = 0

h = $$-$$ 1 $$-$$ k ..... (i)

Given, distance between (h, k) and line 3x + 4y + 2 = 0 is $${1 \over 5}$$.

$$ \therefore $$ $${1 \over 5} = \left| {{{(3h) + (4k) + 2} \over {\sqrt {{3^2} + {4^2}} }}} \right|$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 5} = \left| {{{3( - 1 - k) + 4k + 2} \over {\sqrt {25} }}} \right|$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 5} = \pm \left( {{{ - 3 - 3k + 4k + 2} \over 5}} \right)$$
